theory.intro: The **point of intersection** of two lines is where they cross. Its coordinates are the one pair of $x$ and $y$ values that lie on **both** lines at once, so they make both equations true at the same time.
facts: Read a point of intersection as an ordered pair $(x,y)$: the $x$-value first, then the $y$-value.
The point of intersection lies on both lines, so its coordinates make **both** equations true.
To verify, substitute the point into each equation separately; it must check in both, not just one.
Two lines with the **same gradient** are parallel: they never cross, so there is no point of intersection.

foundation.question_text:
[tikz]
\begin{tikzpicture}\begin{axis}[width=6cm,height=5.6cm,axis lines=middle,axis line style={-{Stealth}},xmin=-2,xmax=6,ymin=-3,ymax=7,xtick={-2,-1,...,6},ytick={-3,-1,...,7},minor y tick num=1,grid=both,clip=false,scaled ticks=false,ticklabel style={font=\small,/pgf/number format/fixed},xlabel={$x$},ylabel={$y$}]\addplot[thick,blue!70!black,<->,domain=-1.8:5.8,samples=2] {x+1};\addplot[thick,red!75!black,dashed,<->,domain=-1.8:5.8,samples=2] {-x+5};\end{axis}\end{tikzpicture}
[/tikz]
The graph shows $y=x+1$ and $y=-x+5$. Find their point of intersection.
foundation.solution_text:
[tikz]
\begin{tikzpicture}\begin{axis}[width=6cm,height=5.6cm,axis lines=middle,axis line style={-{Stealth}},xmin=-2,xmax=6,ymin=-3,ymax=7,xtick={-2,-1,...,6},ytick={-3,-1,...,7},minor y tick num=1,grid=both,clip=false,scaled ticks=false,ticklabel style={font=\small,/pgf/number format/fixed},xlabel={$x$},ylabel={$y$}]\addplot[thick,blue!70!black,<->,domain=-1.8:5.8,samples=2] {x+1};\addplot[thick,red!75!black,dashed,<->,domain=-1.8:5.8,samples=2] {-x+5};\addplot[only marks,mark=*,mark size=2.5pt] coordinates {(2,3)};\node[above right,fill=white,inner sep=1pt] at (axis cs:2,3) {$(2,3)$};\end{axis}\end{tikzpicture}
[/tikz]
$(x,y)=(2,3)$

The graph shows $y=x-4$ and $y=-2x+5$ crossing at $(3,-1)$. Verify that this point lies on both lines.
foundation.solution_text:
[tikz]
\begin{tikzpicture}\begin{axis}[width=6cm,height=5.6cm,axis lines=middle,axis line style={-{Stealth}},xmin=-2,xmax=5,ymin=-5,ymax=6,xtick={-2,-1,...,5},ytick={-5,-3,...,5},minor y tick num=1,grid=both,clip=false,scaled ticks=false,ticklabel style={font=\small,/pgf/number format/fixed},xlabel={$x$},ylabel={$y$}]\addplot[thick,blue!70!black,<->,domain=-0.8:4.8,samples=2] {x-4};\addplot[thick,red!75!black,dashed,<->,domain=-0.4:4.4,samples=2] {-2*x+5};\addplot[only marks,mark=*,mark size=2.5pt] coordinates {(3,-1)};\node[below right,fill=white,inner sep=1pt] at (axis cs:3,-1) {$(3,-1)$};\end{axis}\end{tikzpicture}
[/tikz]
$3-4=-1$
$-2(3)+5=-1$
$(3,-1)$ lies on both lines, so it is the point of intersection.

development.solution_text:
[tikz]
\begin{tikzpicture}\begin{axis}[width=6cm,height=5.6cm,axis lines=middle,axis line style={-{Stealth}},xmin=-1,xmax=6,ymin=-3,ymax=8,xtick={-1,0,...,6},ytick={-3,-1,...,7},minor y tick num=1,grid=both,clip=false,scaled ticks=false,ticklabel style={font=\small,/pgf/number format/fixed},xlabel={$x$},ylabel={$y$}]\addplot[thick,blue!70!black,<->,domain=-0.8:5.8,samples=2] {x-1};\addplot[thick,red!75!black,dashed,<->,domain=0.6:4.4,samples=2] {-2*x+8};\addplot[only marks,mark=*,mark size=2.5pt] coordinates {(3,2)};\node[above right,fill=white,inner sep=1pt] at (axis cs:3,2) {$(3,2)$};\end{axis}\end{tikzpicture}
[/tikz]
$y=x-1:\quad (0,-1),(3,2)$
$y=-2x+8:\quad (0,8),(3,2)$
$(x,y)=(3,2)$
$3-1=2$
$-2(3)+8=2$
$(3,2)$ satisfies both equations.

development.solution_text:
[tikz]
\begin{tikzpicture}\begin{axis}[width=6cm,height=5.6cm,axis lines=middle,axis line style={-{Stealth}},xmin=-4,xmax=3,ymin=-4,ymax=6,xtick={-4,-3,...,3},ytick={-3,-1,...,5},minor y tick num=1,grid=both,clip=false,scaled ticks=false,ticklabel style={font=\small,/pgf/number format/fixed},xlabel={$x$},ylabel={$y$}]\addplot[thick,blue!70!black,<->,domain=-2.8:1.8,samples=2] {2*x+2};\addplot[thick,red!75!black,dashed,<->,domain=-3.8:2.8,samples=2] {-x-1};\addplot[only marks,mark=*,mark size=2.5pt] coordinates {(-1,0)};\node[above left,fill=white,inner sep=1pt] at (axis cs:-1,0) {$(-1,0)$};\end{axis}\end{tikzpicture}
[/tikz]
$y=2x+2:\quad (0,2),(-1,0)$
$y=-x-1:\quad (0,-1),(-1,0)$
$(x,y)=(-1,0)$
$2(-1)+2=0$
$-(-1)-1=0$
$(-1,0)$ satisfies both equations.

mastery.question_text:
[tikz]
\begin{tikzpicture}\begin{axis}[width=6cm,height=5.6cm,axis lines=middle,axis line style={-{Stealth}},xmin=-2,xmax=5,ymin=-4,ymax=7,xtick={-2,-1,...,5},ytick={-4,-2,...,6},minor y tick num=1,grid=both,clip=false,scaled ticks=false,ticklabel style={font=\small,/pgf/number format/fixed},xlabel={$x$},ylabel={$y$}]\addplot[thick,blue!70!black,<->,domain=-1.8:4.8,samples=2] {x+1} node[pos=0.92,right,text=blue!70!black] {$A$};\addplot[thick,red!75!black,dashed,<->,domain=-1.8:4.8,samples=2] {-x+5} node[pos=0.08,above,text=red!75!black] {$B$};\addplot[thick,green!50!black,dotted,<->,domain=0.6:4.4,samples=2] {2*x-4} node[pos=0.92,right,text=green!50!black] {$C$};\end{axis}\end{tikzpicture}
[/tikz]
Lines $A:y=x+1$, $B:y=-x+5$ and $C:y=2x-4$ are shown. Find and verify the point of intersection of lines $B$ and $C$.
mastery.solution_text:
[tikz]
\begin{tikzpicture}\begin{axis}[width=6cm,height=5.6cm,axis lines=middle,axis line style={-{Stealth}},xmin=-2,xmax=5,ymin=-4,ymax=7,xtick={-2,-1,...,5},ytick={-4,-2,...,6},minor y tick num=1,grid=both,clip=false,scaled ticks=false,ticklabel style={font=\small,/pgf/number format/fixed},xlabel={$x$},ylabel={$y$}]\addplot[thick,blue!70!black,<->,domain=-1.8:4.8,samples=2] {x+1} node[pos=0.92,right,text=blue!70!black] {$A$};\addplot[thick,red!75!black,dashed,<->,domain=-1.8:4.8,samples=2] {-x+5} node[pos=0.08,above,text=red!75!black] {$B$};\addplot[thick,green!50!black,dotted,<->,domain=0.6:4.4,samples=2] {2*x-4} node[pos=0.92,right,text=green!50!black] {$C$};\addplot[only marks,mark=*,mark size=2.5pt] coordinates {(3,2)};\node[above right,fill=white,inner sep=1pt] at (axis cs:3,2) {$(3,2)$};\end{axis}\end{tikzpicture}
[/tikz]
$(x,y)=(3,2)$
$B:\quad -3+5=2$
$C:\quad 2(3)-4=2$
$(3,2)$ satisfies both $B:y=-x+5$ and $C:y=2x-4$.

mastery.question_text:
[tikz]
\begin{tikzpicture}\begin{axis}[width=6cm,height=5.6cm,axis lines=middle,axis line style={-{Stealth}},xmin=-3,xmax=4,ymin=-6,ymax=8,xtick={-3,-2,...,4},ytick={-5,-3,...,7},minor y tick num=1,grid=both,clip=false,scaled ticks=false,ticklabel style={font=\small,/pgf/number format/fixed},xlabel={$x$},ylabel={$y$}]\addplot[thick,blue!70!black,<->,domain=-2.8:3.2,samples=2] {2*x+1};\addplot[thick,red!75!black,dashed,<->,domain=-1.4:3.8,samples=2] {2*x-3};\end{axis}\end{tikzpicture}
[/tikz]
The graph shows $y=2x+1$ and $y=2x-3$. Do these lines have a point of intersection? Explain.
mastery.solution_text:
[tikz]
\begin{tikzpicture}\begin{axis}[width=6cm,height=5.6cm,axis lines=middle,axis line style={-{Stealth}},xmin=-3,xmax=4,ymin=-6,ymax=8,xtick={-3,-2,...,4},ytick={-5,-3,...,7},minor y tick num=1,grid=both,clip=false,scaled ticks=false,ticklabel style={font=\small,/pgf/number format/fixed},xlabel={$x$},ylabel={$y$}]\addplot[thick,blue!70!black,<->,domain=-2.8:3.2,samples=2] {2*x+1};\addplot[thick,red!75!black,dashed,<->,domain=-1.4:3.8,samples=2] {2*x-3};\end{axis}\end{tikzpicture}
[/tikz]
Both lines have gradient $2$.
Equal gradients means the lines are parallel.
No, there is no point of intersection: parallel lines never meet.

mastery.question_text:
[tikz]
\begin{tikzpicture}\begin{axis}[width=6cm,height=5.6cm,axis lines=middle,axis line style={-{Stealth}},xmin=-4,xmax=3,ymin=-3,ymax=6,xtick={-4,-3,...,3},ytick={-3,-1,...,5},minor y tick num=1,grid=both,clip=false,scaled ticks=false,ticklabel style={font=\small,/pgf/number format/fixed},xlabel={$x$},ylabel={$y$}]\addplot[thick,blue!70!black,<->,domain=-3.8:2.4,samples=2] {x+3};\addplot[thick,red!75!black,dashed,<->,domain=-3.8:2.4,samples=2] {-x+1};\addplot[only marks,mark=x,mark size=4pt] coordinates {(1,4)};\node[above right,fill=white,inner sep=1pt] at (axis cs:1,4) {$(1,4)$};\end{axis}\end{tikzpicture}
[/tikz]
A learner says $y=x+3$ and $y=-x+1$ intersect at $(1,4)$ because $(1,4)$ lies on $y=x+3$. Use the graph and both equations to give the correct point of intersection.
mastery.solution_text:
[tikz]
\begin{tikzpicture}\begin{axis}[width=6cm,height=5.6cm,axis lines=middle,axis line style={-{Stealth}},xmin=-4,xmax=3,ymin=-3,ymax=6,xtick={-4,-3,...,3},ytick={-3,-1,...,5},minor y tick num=1,grid=both,clip=false,scaled ticks=false,ticklabel style={font=\small,/pgf/number format/fixed},xlabel={$x$},ylabel={$y$}]\addplot[thick,blue!70!black,<->,domain=-3.8:2.4,samples=2] {x+3};\addplot[thick,red!75!black,dashed,<->,domain=-3.8:2.4,samples=2] {-x+1};\addplot[only marks,mark=*,mark size=2.5pt] coordinates {(-1,2)};\node[above right,fill=white,inner sep=1pt] at (axis cs:-1,2) {$(-1,2)$};\end{axis}\end{tikzpicture}
[/tikz]
$1+3=4$
$-(1)+1=0\ne4$
$(x,y)=(-1,2)$
$-1+3=2$
$-(-1)+1=2$
The learner is wrong; the point of intersection is $(-1,2)$, which satisfies both equations.
